23
Contagion: Journal of Violence, Mimesis, and Culture, Vol. 18, 2011, pp. 23–38. ISSN 1075-7201.
© Michigan State University Board of Trustees. All rights reserved.
A Conversation with René Girard
(August 2006/May 2007)
Phil Rose
York University, Toronto, Canada
pr: As you know, I’ve recently att empted to connect your thought to the intel-
lectual tradition of media ecology. Do you have any thoughts regarding that
portrayal?
rg: Well, I felt that you say many things with which I agree, and of course the
tradition which provides your general view is a litt le alien to me. How well
known is it? Is it for these people that you want to interview me, for the people
who belong to that tradition?
pr: Well, to answer your ﬁrst question, Christine Nystrom aptly describes
media ecology as the study of the interactions between communication forms,
technologies, techniques and processes, and human thought, feeling, value, and
behavior, but it is not very well known in fact as a “school” of thought. Read-
ers, however, might be aware of the work of scholars associated with it, such as
Lewis Mumford, Harold Innis, Marshall McLuhan, Jacques Ellul, Walter Ong,
Jack Goody, and Neil Postman.1 It’s Postman, actually, who can properly be seen
to be an “exponent” of the tradition, founding at New York University the ﬁrst
and only graduate program dedicated to its formal study. But you refer in your
This work originally appeared in Contagion 18, 2011, published by Michigan State University Press.

24
Phil Rose
own work to thinkers such as Gregory Bateson and Kenneth Burke, who are
also associated with the media ecology tradition.
With regard to your second question, I suppose it’s probably largely for
myself that I want to interview you, because there are other things that I’m
interested in that fall outside of the particular piece that I’ve writt en concern-
ing your work. But I think it will be of interest to others as well, including
“Girardians.”
rg: Perhaps my general unfamiliarity with the tradition may limit the scope of
my answers. Probably you can direct your questions in such a way that you can
tell me if there are any problems, in order to improve the relationship with the
other tradition.
pr: In “René Girard as Media Ecologist,”2 I was primarily interested in illustrat-
ing two things. First, the relevance of mimetic theory for any scholar of commu-
nication, particularly media ecologists, who generally share the breadth of your
anthropological perspective. But then I also wanted to illustrate, of course, how
media ecological observations bring eff ective nuance to the mimetic theory.
A couple of weeks ago I had dinner with my master’s thesis supervisor—
he’s actually a scholar of Gerard Manley Hopkins—and I was telling him
about your ideas. He was both curious and a litt le unsett led about the idea of
human cultural origins beginning in violence, and he was asking, what does
René Girard say about Eden and the Fall of Mankind? And it occurred to me
aft erwards that the mimetic theory really begins, I think, with the Fall. Does
it not?
rg: Yes, it would be true. But from a Christian point of view one might say that
mimetic violence can be regarded as an interpretation of the original sin. It ﬁts
very well in the sense that it would account for the fact that everybody is part
of the original sin, even if not a part in actuality of committ ing a speciﬁc action.
Th e very fact that mimetic elements are always present in human action and
interaction implies a possibility of rivalry and violence which is common to all
people and which is not dependent on any sort of speciﬁc action which can be
reported.
pr: You’ve writt en in Th ings Hidden since the Foundation of the World [1978] that
by invoking the notion of metaphysical desire, you’re not in any way giving in to
metaphysics. And you explain that notions such as honor and prestige have no
tangible reality, but they’re created by rivalry.
This work originally appeared in Contagion 18, 2011, published by Michigan State University Press.

A Conversation with René Girard
25
rg: Well, metaphysical desire is an anti-metaphysical notion, in the sense that
this desire is always a mirage. Did you think that I belong to the school that
denies metaphysics completely . . . Derrida and company . . . deconstruction?
I’m not giving in to the metaphysics of mimetic desire but, of course, it’s not
necessarily true of other types of metaphysics. See what I mean? Th e metaphys-
ics of mimetic desire is always the means to avoid important questions—like
the question of God—to set it completely outside ourselves. Th erefore, in my
view even the denial of metaphysics would be a child of mimetic desire rather
than of metaphysics. My view is not philosophical anyway, but it’s not “anti-
metaphysics.”
pr: You suggest that in the beginning there is a desiring mimesis that is prior
to representation and to att achment to objects. So I’m wondering in a way if
you’re . . .
rg: Well, I’m not sure your formulation is quite ﬁne, because desire demands
an object ﬁrst—to be born. Desire is not something which is built into the
body; appetite is built into the human body; we certainly have appetite. But for
these appetites to become desires they have to ﬁnd a model. In a way, mimetic
desire would be the diff erence between animals and humans. Th ere is no sin in
animals because there isn’t desire.
pr: I guess the gist of my question is that you don’t ever discuss Creation, and
so I wonder . . . and I realize that that is, perhaps, outside the purview of the
scientiﬁc nature of the mimetic theory. . . .
rg: You mean I never discuss Genesis—the Creation chapter. Th at would
be quite true. But I think one of the problems of American—no, not Ameri-
can, that’s probably not right—but of Christianity has been an extraordinary
emphasis on Genesis. My tradition is Roman Catholic and there is not such an
exclusive emphasis on Genesis.
But there can be an emphasis on the diff erence between the beginning in
the Old Testament and the beginning in the New Testament. In the Gospels—
if you go to the Prologue of John—“He came unto his own and his own did not
receive him.”3 It’s Man who expels God.
I don’t want to invite any quarrel between the various Christian churches,
certainly. But I feel it’s a very important thing that Christian discussions of Gen-
esis rarely take into account that, from a purely Christian point of view, there
may be a question posed about the expulsion of Man by God. Or that it needs
This work originally appeared in Contagion 18, 2011, published by Michigan State University Press.

26
Phil Rose
to be interpreted in order to reconcile the two Testaments in the light of the
Prologue of John, which, from a Christian viewpoint, should take precedence.
pr: In much of your work, you’ve oft en mentioned Simone Weil and your
admiration for her. Do you have any thoughts on her ideas of Creation, her
conception of Creation?
rg: Well, she would certainly not be Biblical at all there, and from my view-
point, most generally, she’s too philosophical and Greek. I’m not sure she’s
immediately helpful. But she says very important things, in the sense that she
sees violence in herself in a way which few thinkers, philosophers, or theolo-
gians have done. When she talked about her headaches—she had very violent
headaches—she says that when she suff ered from them, she had a desire when
she was with other people to hit them in the very spots where she herself hurt.
She has formulas like that that are astonishing.
pr: So you don’t necessarily want to highlight her particular conceptions of
Creation or . . .
rg: No, because I think that she’s unfair vis à vis the Bible. And this has to be
explained. She’s unfair regarding the Bible because in a way she’s a very good
student—her main teacher was a man who is a very well-known philosopher
even today, Émile-Auguste Chartier.
He was typical of much middle-of-the-road thinking in the French human-
istic, republican, anti-Christian tradition. So he was absolutely convinced, and
this was in a way derived from Hegelianism, that the Old Testament was still
the violent God and the New Testament was the nonviolent God. Many people
thought the same. She remains truly faithful to her teacher. So I think there
are aspects of her thought that are not necessarily coherent with some of her
intuitions.
And many English-speaking readers of Simone Weil have been prevented
from understanding the best Simone Weil because of things like that.4 And they
are right to stigmatize them, but they are not as important as they think, not
necessarily coherent with some of her insights.
She was a very good student, and in a way she was too faithful to Chartier
without realizing the inconsistency. So Simone Weil has remained a bone of
contention very oft en for reasons that are due to her own contradictions. I
don’t think she should be regarded as having a body of work that would form a
This work originally appeared in Contagion 18, 2011, published by Michigan State University Press.

A Conversation with René Girard
27
single coherent view of the world. Certainly there are aspects of her that come
from Chartier, and yet there are these extremely powerful and original insights
that she derives—most of the time from Greek tragedy, from Greek epic—
which are very much more Biblical than she realizes. Most Jewish readers are
justiﬁably repelled by that prejudice she has against the Bible, and they don’t go
any further. Th ey are not acquainted enough with her. It destroys their desire to
understand her and to understand how superﬁcial ultimately her anti-Biblical
prejudice is.
pr: I had the opportunity recently to watch the conversation you had with
Gianni Vatt imo, which is available on the internet.5 Th ere was a discussion
between you and him about the Ten Commandments. Vatt imo doubted
whether the Ten Commandments had eternal legitimacy and whether they
were still all as pertinent in the present society as they were when they came
into being. You were suggesting that they were signiﬁcant in an absolute sense.
I wonder what you make of the commandment prohibiting the creation of
images, and what we’re to make of a world now full of images.
rg: Th at’s a very good question. Th e question of images in general and their
use in our society would be another way to justify this commandment at a time
when a superﬁcial examination would say, well, it’s outmoded because it applies
directly to the images of the Divine.
pr: I was going to ask you if you are familiar with the scholarly tradition that
Tom Harpur draws upon and celebrates in his recent book called Th e Pagan
Christ: Recovering the Lost Light [2004]. Do you know of this book?
rg: No.
pr: He’s a Canadian author, so I don’t know if the book has received as much
att ention outside of our country, but perhaps you may know something of
the tradition that he draws upon. He draws upon three thinkers primarily, the
British Egyptologists Godfrey Higgins and Gerald Massey, and an American
scholar of comparative religion, mythology, linguistics, and language, Alvin
Boyd Kuhn.6 Harpur draws on their work to argue (and I think it’s quite incon-
clusive really) that the entire body of Christian doctrine is, to quote Kuhn, who
wrote a book called Th e Lost Light (to which Harpur’s title refers), “a revamped
and mutilated Egyptianism.”
This work originally appeared in Contagion 18, 2011, published by Michigan State University Press.

28
Phil Rose
rg: Rightly or wrongly, I would be repelled by that view, and I would probably
not be able to study it properly or dispassionately.
pr: I guess the primary emphasis is the suggestion that Jesus wasn’t in fact his-
torical. Now, to me it seems that your point of view must be that Jesus in fact
was historical.
rg: Yes.
pr: So he wants to emphasize that the Gospels aren’t history, so to speak, and
they’re suggesting . . .
rg: Well, there’s nothing new about that. Much of the second half of the nine-
teenth century had the same starting point.
pr: Right . . . and of course what they’re trying to portray is that Jesus is in large
part modeled aft er Horus in Egyptian culture. Now, I think that there are traces
throughout Harpur’s book that talk about and pinpoint possibly the argument
that you make, which is the origin of the sacred in the violence toward the god
in question. But he seems to be completely oblivious of your work too—and I’d
be interested in how he might reinterpret his ﬁndings in relation to your thesis.
In that regard, can you make any commentary on the myths of Horus, or Ra, or
Osiris and Isis?
rg: To me they are standard examples. Now, the relationship between mythol-
ogy and Christianity to me has absolutely to be mentioned, and the objective
facts as seen from outside are exactly the same. Th is is what modern anthropol-
ogy has rediscovered. It has said that Christianity is only a myth. Th ey don’t
realize that in both cases, what you have is fundamentally a unanimous agree-
ment against a single victim. In mythology this agreement holds. Th is agree-
ment is the truth. Everything in mythology is shaped by the worldview carried
by that agreement.
And what they forget about Christianity is that in Christianity it’s not.
Everybody believes the accusation, but not the Gospels. Th erefore, the repre-
sentation by the text is fundamental. Th at’s what people don’t understand about
my idea of the scapegoat phenomenon, because it’s fused with a vulgar under-
standing of scapegoat, which people try to rectify by going to the signiﬁcant
passages in Th e Golden Bough. But it’s not enough. Th ere is a scapegoat phe-
nomenon only for people who don’t realize there is such a phenomenon. And as
soon as you realize it—in other words, as soon as you say Jesus is the victim of
This work originally appeared in Contagion 18, 2011, published by Michigan State University Press.

A Conversation with René Girard
29
people who don’t really understand anything about myth . . . see what I mean?
When this understanding is there, myth is ﬁnished.
Th erefore, I can say, yes, of course, there is a relationship between these
myths and Christianity, but this is a relationship which is mastered only by the
Gospels. Because we don’t realize—only the Gospels realize—that myth is
the normal misinterpretation of the type of phenomena which the Passion of
Christ is. Jesus, for instance, asks in Matt hew 21:42 (making reference to Psalm
118:22), “Have you never read in the scriptures, ‘Th e stone the builders rejected
has become the cornerstone?’” If you account for that, you have the mimetic
view of the origin of humans in violence, of all humans in violence, which is
fully revealed only in the Passion.
pr: Can you tell me at what point in evolution would the victimage mechanism
have come into play?
rg: It would be the mechanism of hominization. . . . what scholars call homi-
nization.
pr: Th ere seem to be—in the thinking about the development of speech and
language—three diff erent views about when that may have occurred. Do you
have a particular point of view in terms of . . . ?
rg: I have no idea. . . . I think in order to have a view, you’d have to be a researcher
in that ﬁeld.
pr: I was curious about that, since you suggest that the victimage mechanism’s
founding of culture represents also the foundation of symbolic thought. . . .
another reason, incidentally, why I think your work ought to have great rel-
evance to students of communication.
With regard to the three hypotheses concerning the origin of language,
the ﬁrst situates it at the beginning of the Lower Palaeolithic period, about 1.8
million years ago. Th e second locates it during the Middle Palaeolithic period,
approximately 250,000 years ago, and the third (and most likely explanation
in my view) places the origins of speech either during the Upper Palaeolithic
period, 40,000 years ago, or even more recently, about 10,000 years ago dur-
ing the Neolithic period.7 Either way, the birth of symbolic thought must
signiﬁcantly precede the origins of language, I would say.
But switching gears for a moment . . . you mentioned mirror neurons to me
when we were in Ott awa recently. . . .
This work originally appeared in Contagion 18, 2011, published by Michigan State University Press.

30
Phil Rose
rg: Well, I don’t know much more than you do. A group of researchers in
Parma8 have discovered mirror neurons. But I don’t have much to add. You see,
the thing which is very fascinating about mirror neurons is that they are acti-
vated by the act of doing something. But they are equally activated by the act of
seeing this thing being done by someone else. Th at’s the main thing. Absolutely
fascinating, and that you can relate it with the mimetic view is certainly obvious.
But at the same time, I haven’t seen anything more than this. Th is is the funda-
mental thing. If it is true, it’s going to be fundamental. But it would be wrong to
give the impression that it played an important role in my view.
pr: But you think it’s something that may off er some insights?
rg: Yes.
pr: You’ve writt en about how, say, in Th e Scapegoat [1982/1986] your
demystiﬁcation of Machaut, and I suppose this would apply to your demysti-
ﬁcation of myth as well, cannot be considered falsiﬁable in Karl Popper’s sense
of what constitutes science. Does that mean that you consider Popper’s inter-
pretation of . . .
rg: Or that it would be outside of the realm of what Popper calls science,
because we are dealing with human conduct. And there in Machaut you have
a text which obviously talks about persecution of the Jews in circumstances
where the idea that the Jews could really be guilty of what is writt en there makes
no sense.
And more important than that in a way is the introduction to the text by
Ernest Hoepff ner. I think I made it clear in Th e Scapegoat. Hoepff ner’s intro-
duction to the text9 does not mention anti-Semitism and the mechanism of
accusation in language, omits it as something not worth talking about. Th is is
extremely important in my view. Because it is, in a way—even though it cer-
tainly does not demonstrate any anti-Semitism in the man who published the
text and so forth, who was a very honest person. . . .
But one doesn’t talk about these things. And I think that part of the silence
around myth is constituted not only by the essential inability of a people united
in scapegoating to understand what they have done, but in the fact that there
is a general tendency not to talk about these subjects. We know that in a group
of human beings there is such a thing as panic. Fear becomes panic, and very
oft en it will direct itself against the probable cause of that panic. And if someone
This work originally appeared in Contagion 18, 2011, published by Michigan State University Press.

A Conversation with René Girard
31
thinks he has found the probable cause, of course, there will be a tendency for
everyone else to follow.
What makes people sure that they are right is unanimity. Period. Unanim-
ity as such is an argument in favor—can be an argument in favor of the truth.
“Look, they cannot be wrong, since they all say the same thing, and they must
have the truth of the phenomenon.”
pr: So in a way the mimetic theory could never be falsiﬁable in the Popperian
sense. . . .
rg: No.
pr: . . . and so . . . now, I get the impression that you don’t believe—and I’m not
sure I’m understanding Popper properly in saying this—but you don’t believe
that we ought to reserve the word “science” to refer solely to the mathematical
certainty of the natural sciences. . . .
rg: Do you think that the circulation of the blood discovered by Harvey in the
sixteenth century, do you think it’s falsiﬁable today? It has become too evident
for that. To believe in the circulation of the blood is still science, you know? If it
was science in the days of Harvey it is science today. But it has become common
knowledge.
Our common knowledge is not the same as in the sixteenth century; there
are aspects of science that move into the nonfalsiﬁable. For instance, when we
observe human beings doing something and we say this is a scapegoat phe-
nomenon, this character is not guilty. Is it always falsiﬁable? No, it’s not always
falsiﬁable. But in certain instances, we can say the accusation is false. And we
can be absolutely sure of what we are saying. We can stake everything on that
without fear. So is it science? I think there are areas where what the logicians
call common knowledge today should be inserted. Th ere are things which are
common knowledge that are true. Are they scientiﬁc or not?
pr: So would you say that it’s not unsuitable to say, when we talk about our
social sciences, that we are doing science?
rg: I think it’s not unsuitable. Take Western anthropology between 1875 and
1950. When it believed in itself, it believed that it could give a deﬁnition of
religion, a deﬁnition of sacriﬁce, a deﬁnition of the sacred. And later on, the
This work originally appeared in Contagion 18, 2011, published by Michigan State University Press.

32
Phil Rose
Structuralists had proclaimed that you couldn’t deﬁne such things as the sacred
in meaningful terms that would be, therefore, scientiﬁc, in the same sense as an
astronomical or scientiﬁc hypothesis which is completely mathematized.
I think part of the failure of the second half of the twentieth century and of
its loss of heart and dynamism has been due to its excessive skepticism. It has
tended to destroy all possibility of certainty that would not be mathematical
science. But this in my view is completely wrong. And this is absolutely indis-
pensable to the mimetic theory, in the sense that the type of conviction that the
mimetic theory gives to you is of a diff erent nature. But at the same time, it has
conﬁdence in the ability of the human spirit to detect truth, which today much
intellectual enterprise refuses—which I think is very bad.
pr: So as you said, it’s bett er to reintegrate the banal and traditional framework
of scientiﬁc research.
rg: Yes. Th e word “science” should not be reserved to what is completely based
on mathematics and divorced from human perception. People who dedicate
their lives to investigation in the domain of human phenomena know that there
is a type of certainty which is not covered by Popper. We know very well when
we read a novel if we are in the presence of a person who has a profound under-
standing of the human mind.
So the question of mathematical science versus everything else is just nihil-
ism and should be refused. Not in order to diminish the virtues of mathematical
science. Not at all. But in order to say there are areas which cannot be mathema-
tized, cannot be objects of the type of certainty that can be deﬁned with preci-
sion, and which should not be disgraced by being treated as pure impression.
pr: Does scientism still present a signiﬁcant cultural threat?
rg: I think probably that threat is a litt le diminished, though it’s much greater
today in the sense that the earlier scientistic att itudes have done so many things
in the world that today threaten us, or may threaten us. But I think people have
unlearned that kind of earlier uncritical optimism.
pr: So you think it’s somewhat of a reduced threat at the moment, then.
rg: Well, the threat is not reduced, in the sense that it’s running wild in the world
itself, but many people are aware of it now. For instance, I think one should
emphasize that awareness a litt le more in the matt ers related to life. Given what
This work originally appeared in Contagion 18, 2011, published by Michigan State University Press.

A Conversation with René Girard
33
humanity has done with its ability to create explosives and that sort of thing.
To consider that it is safe to do away with all safeguards in the manipulation of
genes and their reproduction shows too much optimism. And one should say
frankly that safeguards are needed, and not only because of absolute principles
but in relation to the safety of the human race today.
pr: About a year ago, you gave an interview for the newslett er of the Interna-
tional Jacques Ellul Society,10 and you said that our technology is like the sor-
cerer’s apprentice, that it threatens us and must be controlled or restrained in
some way. Can you say what type of controls you might have in mind?
rg: No, I don’t have anything. Because at the same time I think . . . well, demo-
cratic governments are still superior. And decisions are still left to individual
persons as well. But we know that our situation is really that of the sorcerer’s
apprentice: more and more people realize it.
pr: It seems to me that laissez-faire in the economic sense has also very oft en
amounted to laissez-faire in the sense of technological development. Do you
think that there has to be some kind of . . . ?
rg: Yes . . .
pr: . . . some orientation towards a more careful appraisal of . . .
rg: . . . much more careful because today we are no longer caught in the alterna-
tives between socialism and free enterprise. We realize that free enterprise has
lots of advantages in the short run [laughs], but we are not sure at all what it’s
doing in the long run. Unfree enterprise is simply no enterprise at all.
pr: Do you have any thoughts on what Dwight Eisenhower’s team called the
military-industrial complex and the threat that that posed to . . . ?
rg: Well, I’m thinking about it very oft en. Eisenhower was oft en despised by
intellectuals because he was not an intellectual himself. But he was a man of
common sense, very practical. And obviously with a very well-organized mind,
because his greatest achievement was the organization of the landing and
operation in Western Europe at the time of 1944. Given the type of man that
Eisenhower was, his training and so forth, this warning is really a magniﬁcent
intellectual achievement. He understood something fundamental, and it was
This work originally appeared in Contagion 18, 2011, published by Michigan State University Press.

34
Phil Rose
in a way the great lesson that he had drawn from his experience as president
of the United States at an important time. Th at’s what he left to us in his last
speech. Everybody saw it then, and they felt that he was not moved by ideologi-
cal prejudice. I think we should pay much more att ention now to what he said
than we do.
pr: Do you have any thoughts on the current situation concerning Israel and
Lebanon, and where this might take us in the future?
rg: I think it’s a very desperate situation. I don’t know if I have anything else
to say. But I tend to be prett y pessimistic, because I strongly feel that the West-
ern powers are responsible, you know, the powers that won the Second World
War are responsible for the existence of enmity. If Israel disappears, if it were
destroyed by force, outright destroyed, it would be such a catastrophe for the
whole world.
pr: I wonder if there’s any such ideas that we can consider in terms of ﬁghting a
just war on terror and whether . . .
rg: I would say personally I’m inclined to believe there is a just war on terror.
Whatever the apocalyptic possibilities there are . . .
pr: Do you have any thoughts on the new doctrine of preemption and the idea
of preemptive war?
rg: I think it’s a very frightening development; it shows that retribution and
vengeance are still around, and it is very disturbing. At the same time, undoubt-
edly, the existence of terrorism is literally a monstrous problem, and I don’t want
to deny its importance. It shows that history is as dangerous as ever, because the
most archaic, barbarian types of violence seem to be back with us at the same
time as the most sophisticated means of destruction which face mankind in this
situation, which is without parallel in the past. So I don’t want to say anything
which is rash; I will just say that it confronts us with a problem of incalculable
dimensions, and that it is one of the most terrifying things in our universe.
From a Christian point of view, I would say that the consequences of this are
eschatological and apocalyptic. You can talk about total destruction rather than
limited destruction as before. But I don’t have any solution. Th is is precisely the
essence of the situation—that there is no solution to it.
This work originally appeared in Contagion 18, 2011, published by Michigan State University Press.

A Conversation with René Girard
35
pr: Your former student Robert Doran was telling me that one of your present
projects is looking at the work of Virginia Woolf. Do you have any other future
projects in mind at the moment that are going to take up your att ention?
rg: I have a historical project which is centered upon a book which is neither a
literary nor a philosophical one, Clausewitz’s On War [1832]. Th is book should
be an answer to the people who say, “Isn’t it a weakness of the mimetic theory
that it comes from literature?”11
I think one of the deﬁnitions one could give of Clausewitz’s On War—
whatever the book is, which is many things—is that the author was an Enlight-
enment man inevitably, writing at the beginning of the nineteenth century, even
though he was politically reactionary. But at the same time, even though he’s
an Enlightenment man, therefore believing in Reason, he’s writing essentially
about bad human relations, human relations of conﬂict.
It is a fascinating book because it’s a mystery. It’s an unﬁnished book,
although Clausewitz spent most of his adult life on it. Its consequences have
been incredible, and the misunderstanding of him also incredible. But if you
look at it, at the ﬁrst chapter, which is the only one that Clausewitz said was
complete, you will see that it’s a kind of mimetic deﬁnition of war which he
called “the chameleon.” Th e word “imitation” is not there in that ﬁrst chapter,
but war is deﬁned as a rise to extremes, and it’s constantly deﬁned in terms of
reciprocity.
His style is a philosophical German style, but when people say it’s Hege-
lian—no, I would say it’s as anti-Hegelian as possible, because it’s a view of
violence which is mimetic. So even though Clausewitz, in a way, has an att itude
which refers back to a kind of a chivalric att itude towards war—“No gentleman
is a complete gentleman if he doesn’t have the experience of war”—You know?
Th at sort of thing. Th ere is a kind of warrior’s humanism which is very Prussian-
ized . . . traditional. At the same time, he is so full of prophecy of the forthcom-
ing war—he sees all war as a single war, which is a war rising to extremes. And
in that constant rise to the extremes he sees technology itself, the technology of
war, the instruments, so it’s very modern.
Th at’s why people have been writing—like the French sociologist Ray-
mond Aron—of Clausewitz in connection with the atomic age. And I think
it’s a good starting point for an essay on the role of war from Napoleon to the
present and the future. Because Clausewitz’s view of Napoleon is extremely
vast. But he’s totally mimetic. Napoleon he hates so cordially that he loves him,
and he sees him as the man who rescues war from the tendency to corrupt war
This work originally appeared in Contagion 18, 2011, published by Michigan State University Press.

36
Phil Rose
itself, if I may say, in the eighteenth century: “negotiations are more important
than war” and that sort of thing.
So there is something aesthetically very powerful in Clausewitz—some-
thing terrifying about him, because in a sense he gives a kind of eulogy of war
with all sorts of forebodings that look to the present time—to the terrifying
situation in which we are.
pr: It sounds as though your analysis is going to concentrate as much on the
present as on his starting point?
rg: Yes, Clausewitz has had a great inﬂuence on the European conﬂicts. And
that’s very curious, you know. Th e people who write on war in England, for
instance, I think were very profoundly inﬂuenced even in reaction against
Clausewitz. Th e German batt le plan of 1870 against France was pure Clausewitz,
or thought it was. Because in military circles there is a lot of misinterpretation of
Clausewitz. I think that’s part of the irony of the whole thing. But he’s something
which remains unexploited and I think may or may not—it’s questionable, we’ll
see what the ﬁnal product is—enable us to talk about the present diff erently
and about war in general and about Europe.
pr: Sounds like a great project.
NOTES
1. Th e American public intellectual Lewis Mumford (1895–1990) was inﬂuenced by the
notion of human ecology as articulated by the Scott ish biologist Patrick Geddes, and
he was also signiﬁcantly inﬂuenced by the work of the University of Chicago economist
Th orstein Veblen. Mumford emphasizes the development of the machine and machine
civilization, and among his most enduring writings are Technics and Civilization (New York:
Harcourt, Brace and Company, 1934), Th e Myth of the Machine, Vol. 1: Technics and Human
Development (New York: Harcourt, Brace and World, 1967), and Th e Myth of the Machine,
Vol. II: Th e Pentagon of Power (Harcourt, Brace, Jovanovich, 1974). Also, Patrick Geddes
made a direct impression on the work of the Canadian scholar Harold Innis (1894–1952),
while inﬂuencing him indirectly through the University of Chicago sociologists Robert E.
Park, Earnest W. Burgess, and Roderick D. McKenzie. Innis’s historical and sociological
approaches to the development of communication technologies in Western civilization
are well represented in Empire and Communications (Oxford: Clarendon Press,1950) and
Th e Bias of Communications (Toronto: University of Toronto Press, 1951). Scholars oft en
connect him to the “Toronto School of Communications” in conjunction with Marshall
McLuhan (1911–80), perhaps the most widely known ﬁgure in the media ecology tradition.
McLuhan explores issues of communication in relation to ideas concerning acoustic and
visual space in his primary texts, Th e Gutenberg Galaxy: Th e Making of Typographic Man
This work originally appeared in Contagion 18, 2011, published by Michigan State University Press.

A Conversation with René Girard
37
(Toronto: University of Toronto Press, 1962), Understanding Media: Th e Extensions of Man
(New York: McGraw-Hill, 1964) and Laws of Media: Th e New Science (Toronto: University
of Toronto Press, 1988), the last-mentioned work completed with his son Eric. Jacques Ellul
(1912–94) authored Th e Technological Society (New York: Knopf, 1964) and Propaganda:
Th e Formation of Men’s Att itudes (New York: Knopf, 1965), in addition to many theologically
oriented works, and is best known for his analysis of technique. Walter Ong (1912–2003)
completed his M.A. thesis under the direction of McLuhan, and introduced the terms “pri-
mary and secondary orality” in order to distinguish cultures without writing from those
whose information environments have integrated electronic communications and sound
recording techniques. Ong is perhaps best known for Th e Presence of the Word: Some Pro-
legomena for Cultural and Religious History (New Haven: Yale University Press, 1967) and
Orality and Literacy: Th e Technologizing of the Word (London; New York: Methuen, 1982).
Th e British anthropologist Jack Goody (b. 1919) has also made signiﬁcant contributions to
orality-literacy thinking, particularly in Th e Domestication of the Savage Mind (Cambridge;
New York: Cambridge University Press, 1977), while Postman’s inﬂuence has been second
probably only to that of McLuhan, especially with books such as Th e Disappearance of
Childhood (New York: Vintage Books, 1994), Amusing Ourselves to Death: Public Discourse
in the Age of Show Business (New York: Penguin, 1985), and Technopoly: Th e Surrender of
Culture to Technology (New York: Knopf, 1992).
2. Phil Rose, “René Girard as Media Ecologist,” in Valuation and Media Ecology: Ethics, Mor-
als, and Laws, ed. Corey Anton (Creskill, New Jersey: Hampton Press, 2010), 91–122.
3. John 1:11.
4. Girard cites as the best example T. S. Eliot, who wrote the preface to Weil’s Th e Need for
Roots: Prelude to a Declaration of Duties toward Mankind, trans. Arthur Willis (New York:
G. P. Putnam, 1952).
5. See the recently released book of their deliberations, Gianni Vatt imo and René Girard,
Christianity, Truth, and Weakening Faith: A Dialogue, ed. Pierpaolo Antonello (New York:
Columbia University Press, 2010).
6. Tom Harpur is a former Anglican minister and professor of Greek and New Testament at
the University of Toronto, as well as a writer and broadcaster. Harpur draws very much on
the work of Kuhn (1880–1963), who received his Ph.D. from Columbia University aft er
completing his dissertation on theosophy. Kuhn was in turn heavily inﬂuenced by Higgins
(1772–1883), a Cambridge-trained archaeologist best known for his 1833 book Anacalypsis:
An Inquiry into the Origin of Languages, Nations and Religions (Kila, MT: Kessinger Publish-
ing, 1997), and Massey (1828–1907), whose most proliﬁc work is Ancient Egypt: Th e Light
of the World (Kila, MT: Kessinger Publishing, 1907/2001).
7. For an outline of the ﬁrst, “long” view of the beginnings of language, see Richard Leakey,
Th e Origin of Humankind (New York: Basic Books, 1994). For the second, “middle” view,
see Steven Mithen, Th e Prehistory of the Mind (New York: Th ames and Hudson, 1999); L.
Aiello and R.I. M. Dunbar, “Neocortex Size, Group Size and the Evolution of Language,”
Current Anthropology 34 (1993): 184–93; Stephen Pinker, Th e Language Instinct (New York:
William Morrow, 1994); or the work of Philip Lieberman. For the “short” view of language
acquisition, see Julian Jaynes, Th e Origins of Consciousness in the Breakdown of the Bicameral
Mind (Boston: Houghton Mifﬂin, 1976); Kristen Malmkjaer, “Origin of Language,” in Th e
Linguistics Encyclopedia, ed. K. Malmkjaer and J. Anderson (New York: Routledge, 1991),
324–29; and Robert Albrecht, “Preface to Homer: Th e Role of Music in the Acquisition
and Evolution of Speech,” Th e Speech Communication Annual 14 (2000): 81–105.
